summary: The Trinity is the Christian doctrine that the one God exists as three coequal, coeternal persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. It affirms that God is one in essence (ousia) and three in person (hypostasis), neither dividing the divine substance nor confusing the persons.
description: The doctrine of the Trinity holds that there is one God, and that this one God is Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, three distinct persons who share fully and equally in a single divine essence. Christianity does not present this as three gods, nor as a single person acting in three roles, but as one being (Greek ousia, Latin substantia) subsisting eternally in three persons (Greek hypostaseis, Latin personae). The doctrine took its normative conciliar form in the fourth century. The Council of Nicaea (325) confessed the Son as homoousios, of the same essence as the Father, against the Arian teaching that the Son was a created being; the Council of Constantinople (381) extended this to the Holy Spirit, producing the creed commonly called the Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed. The Cappadocian Fathers (Basil of Caesarea, Gregory of Nazianzus, Gregory of Nyssa) supplied the classic conceptual vocabulary distinguishing the one ousia from the three hypostaseis. A later point of division between the Latin West and the Greek East concerns the procession of the Spirit and the Filioque clause (whether the Spirit proceeds from the Father alone, or from the Father and the Son), which remains a church-dividing question between Eastern Orthodox and Western churches. Within the tradition the doctrine is understood not as a speculative puzzle but as the summary of how God is disclosed in the biblical narrative of Father, Son, and Spirit.
editorial_notes: This entry states the doctrine on Christianity's own terms first (neutrality principle #6): one essence, three persons, in the Nicene-Constantinopolitan formulation, without adjudicating the Filioque dispute, which is named as a live division rather than resolved. Scope is deliberately introductory; the Cappadocian ousia/hypostasis distinction and the Filioque are each candidates for their own entries. Sourcing triangulates three lenses per neutrality principles #8-9: academic history of doctrine (Pelikan vol. 1), classical primary conciliar and patristic material (the Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed; Athanasius, whose anti-Arian defense underlies Nicene orthodoxy), and a modern Christian systematic voice (McGrath's textbook). No encyclopedias or tertiary sources are cited. Confidence is medium: the primary conciliar text is definitive, but the secondary works' relevant chapters were not read line-by-line. The cross-tradition link to tawhid follows ADR-0005: it asserts a shared QUESTION (the oneness and nature of God), states the DIFFERENCE (absolute oneness admitting no persons vs. one God in three persons), and neither equates nor ranks the two. Several Christian relation targets (arianism, council-of-nicaea) are forward references to entities not yet created in this dataset, as original-sin already notes for its own targets.

relations.note: Both address the oneness and nature of God. Tawhid affirms an absolute divine oneness that admits no internal distinction of persons and treats the association of any partner with God (shirk) as the cardinal error; the Trinity affirms one God in three coequal persons, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, sharing one essence. The traditions frame the same question, the unity of God, and give incompatible answers; this link asserts neither equivalence nor superiority.
The confession of the Son as fully God is bound up with the Trinitarian confession; the Nicene homoousios grounds the incarnational claim.
Nicene Trinitarianism was defined against the Arian teaching that the Son is a created, subordinate being.
The 325 council confessed the Son as homoousios with the Father.

historical_period.note: Rooted in the New Testament naming of Father, Son, and Spirit; given normative conciliar form at Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381), with the Cappadocian Fathers supplying the technical vocabulary.

sources.citation: Pelikan, Jaroslav. The Christian Tradition: A History of the Development of Doctrine, Vol. 1: The Emergence of the Catholic Tradition (100-600). Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1971. Traces the fourth-century development of Trinitarian dogma, the Arian controversy, and the settlements of Nicaea and Constantinople.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: The Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed (Symbol of Nicaea-Constantinople), promulgated at the First Council of Nicaea (325) and the First Council of Constantinople (381). Primary conciliar text confessing the Son as homoousios with the Father and the divinity of the Holy Spirit.

Athanasius of Alexandria. On the Incarnation (De Incarnatione Verbi Dei). Trans. John Behr, Popular Patristics Series 44b. Yonkers, NY: St Vladimir's Seminary Press, 2011. Fourth-century primary text defending the full divinity of the Son against Arian subordinationism, foundational to Nicene Trinitarian orthodoxy.
sources.license: Proprietary translation; classical Greek text public domain. Cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: McGrath, Alister E. Christian Theology: An Introduction. Oxford: Wiley-Blackwell. A standard modern Christian systematic-theology textbook whose chapters on the doctrine of God expound the one-essence-three-persons formulation and the Filioque question. Added as the modern Christian scholarly lens.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.
